tikz-graphtheory-dor1-55-c69e3894: Sixteen-vertex 4-by-4 grid graph with mostly perimeter edges plus a few interior verticals and one horizontal; the exercise asks the student to eulerize the graph using as few edge duplications as possible and then find an Euler circuit.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-56-7c490159: Eighteen-vertex graph laid out on an irregular grid representing a neighborhood street map; the city crew exercise asks the student to find an efficient inspection route by eulerizing the graph and then finding an Euler circuit.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-57-dafe33e7: Five-vertex graph with corners at (0,0), (2,0), (0,2), (2,2) plus a center node (1,1); edges form a triangle on the bottom and a Z-shape through the center, asking whether a Hamiltonian circuit exists.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-58-75e6f7de: Bipartite graph K_{3,3}: three black-dot vertices on the bottom row at x=0, 1.5, 3 connected to three on the top row at the same x-coordinates by all nine cross edges; asks whether a Hamiltonian circuit exists.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-59-3c3b05bf: Seven-vertex graph drawn as a 3-by-2 lattice plus a top peak at (1,2); edges form the perimeter of the lattice with two interior diagonals and a triangle at the top, presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-60-fda44dcb: Eight-vertex graph: outer square at (0,0), (2,0), (2,2), (0,2) and inner square at (.7,.7), (1.4,.7), (1.4,1.4), (.7,1.4), with corresponding outer-corner-to-inner-corner spokes; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-61-db94e8ec: Bipartite graph K_{3,2}: three vertices on the bottom row at x=0, 1.5, 3 each connected by an edge to two vertices on top at (0.75,2) and (2.25,2), six edges total; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-62-68959709: Six-vertex graph: vertex (1,0) sits at the center linked by five edges to (0,0), (2,0), (1,1), (1,-1), and (2,-1), with additional edges forming triangles above and below; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-63-40153fd7: Weighted graph on five labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E in a pentagonal layout, fully connected by ten edges with dollar-cost labels (e.g. CD=$5.6, CA=$5.1, BC=$4.3, AE=$4.4, CE=$4.0); used to plan a minimum-cost sonet ring.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-64-d29521d8: Bipartite graph K_{3,3} drawn as three black-dot vertices on the bottom and three on top, connected by all nine crossing edges; the exercise asks for which n a graph built this way has Euler or Hamiltonian circuits.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-65-265d922c: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid with horizontal edges weighted 2, 3, 2 and vertical edges weighted 1; asks the student to eulerize the graph in the most efficient way considering the edge weights.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-66-f94e9071: Sixteen-vertex 4-by-4 grid with horizontal edges weighted 2, 4, 2 and vertical edges weighted 1; asks the student to eulerize the graph in the most efficient way considering the edge weights.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-67-58bc5357: Pentagonal weighted graph on labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E with nine edges and integer weights (e.g. CD=26, AC=24, BC=44, AB=22, AE=30, DE=28); asks the student to eulerize it minimizing total added weight.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-68-b5c9652c: Pentagonal weighted graph on labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E with nine edges and small integer weights (e.g. EC=12, AE=9, DE=13, BE=4, CD=11, AC=8, AB=10, AD=5, BD=6); asks the student to eulerize it minimizing total added weight.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-69-e486d08d: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid graph with weighted horizontal, vertical, and two diagonal edges (e.g. weights 5, 6, 7, 4 horizontally; 3, 2, 1 vertically; diagonal (2,0)-(6,2)=3 and (2,2)-(6,0)=2); two vertices have odd degree, used to motivate the Chinese Postman Problem.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-70-8b8c4c39: Four-vertex undirected graph with B at (0,0), C at (4,0), D at (2,2), A at (2,4) and five edges BC, BD, BA, CD, CA forming a kite; left panel of an example illustrating an Euler path CABDCB.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-71-eb4b8064: Same four-vertex kite graph (B, C, D, A) as the left panel, but with directed arrows along the Euler path CABDCB; edges are numbered 1 through 5 in the order they are traversed.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-72-31687411: Six-vertex graph with vertices A, B, C, D, E, F in a roughly diamond layout and nine undirected edges; the example asks the student to find an Euler circuit such as ADEACEFCBA starting and ending at A.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-73-b9fc78c4: Same six-vertex graph as the previous panel, redrawn with directed arrows tracing the Euler circuit AECABCFEDA and edges numbered 1 through 9 in traversal order.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-74-52f8addf: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), C(4,0), D(2,2), A(2,4), E(6,2) and edges CE, BD, BA, CD, CA, plus a bent edge from A to C; vertices D and E have odd degree, A and C have degree 4, B has degree 2, illustrating Euler's path-but-not-circuit theorem.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-75-f7587543: Eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with nodes A through R laid out on a roughly grid-like map of streets; reused from earlier in the chapter to ask whether an Euler circuit exists for the lawn inspector.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-76-390ec09c: Same eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with the eight vertices of odd degree (D, B, G, N, O, H, J, P) highlighted as filled yellow circles, showing why no Euler circuit exists.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-77-a5782611: The eighteen-vertex housing-development graph redrawn with every street represented by two parallel edges (one straight, one bent) so that every vertex now has even degree, illustrating that the snowplow's two-sides-of-the-street graph does have an Euler circuit.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-78-30fbb21c: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), A(1,2), C(1,-2), E(3,-1), D(3,1) and six edges; the original input graph for the worked Fleury's algorithm example that finds Euler circuit ADEBDCA starting at A.
